summary: Jess Frazelle celebrates the Unix pipe as a brilliant piece of software design that embodies the Unix philosophy of making programs that do one thing well and can be combined with others. She traces the concept back to Doug McIlroy's 1964 memo proposing ways to 'couple programs like garden hose.' The post argues that this design pattern enables creativity and extensibility by allowing simple tools to become building blocks for solving complex problems the original authors never anticipated.
key_points: The pipe operator allows output from one program to become input for another
The concept predates Unix, originating in Doug McIlroy's 1964 memo
Unix philosophy emphasizes doing one thing well and expecting output to become input for unknown future programs
Simple tools combined via pipes become more powerful than monolithic programs
Printing output to terminal is actually the 'special case' - redirection is the true power
money_quotes: We should have some ways of coupling programs like garden hose--screw in another segment when it becomes necessary to massage data in another way.
What I love about Unix is the philosophy of 'do one thing well' and 'expect the output of every program to become the input to another'.
When a user redirects the output of cat via a | to some other program, cat becomes so much more than what the original author intended.
The original author of cat does not even need to know about the larger plan. That is the beauty of the | it allows for solving problems by combining small, simple programs together.
I love software design that enables creativity, values simplicity, and doesn't put users in a box.
key_insight: The Unix pipe represents brilliant software design because it allows simple, single-purpose programs to become building blocks for solving complex problems their original authors never imagined.
